$comment: The design system, as a file an agent cannot argue with.
iris's generic critique asks 'are you consistent with yourself' — which catches
drift but cannot tell you what you should have chosen. This says what you chose.
Point iris at it and every value that is not in here is named, with the nearest
one that is: '13px is not in your type scale — 12 or 14'.
That is the whole mechanism by which agent design gets good: not more taste,
fewer decisions. A model writing CSS a rule at a time cannot remember what it
picked ten lines ago. It does not have to, if the answer is in a file.
Extract a starting point from a page you already like:  iris tokens <url>
name: tools-for-agents
$game: A canvas is ONE element with ONE colour as far as the DOM knows, so every check
above is blind to a game. Games got no design review at all — which is exactly why
agent-written games look the way they do: 'red', then '#ff0000', then 'crimson',
across two hundred lines, and nobody ever chose any of them.
So declare what a colour is ALLOWED TO MEAN. iris reads the pixels the game
actually drew and names every colour that is not one of these, with the role it
`tolerance` is how far a drawn pixel may sit from a role and still BE that role
(redmean distance, 0-765). It does double duty: two roles closer together than the
tolerance are ambiguous by construction — a pixel could not be attributed to either
— which is also the definition of a player who cannot tell the thing they chase
from the thing that kills them.
